pattern: Repeated division by the negative base works like ordinary base conversion, but a negative remainder must be lifted to zero or one by borrowing, which nudges the quotient up by one.
hints: Just like converting to base two you peel off one digit at a time with the remainder of division by the base, but here the base is negative two.
Division by a negative base can yield a negative remainder; a digit must be zero or one, so when the remainder is negative add the base's magnitude to it and compensate the quotient.
Loop while n is nonzero: take r = n mod 2 forced into {0,1}, prepend r, then set n = (n - r) / -2; if n started at zero the answer is the single character zero.

edge_inputs.description: zero maps to a single zero digit
two needs the borrow to produce three digits

stress.description: random values across the whole range
